---
title: "A deviance-bias correction for the Q-function under congenial multiple imputation"
subtitle: "With applications to likelihood-ratio model comparison and information-criterion model selection"
author:
  - name: Marcus Waldman
    orcid: 0000-0000-0000-0000
    affiliation: University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus
    role: Human prompter (ORCID-verified)
    email: marcus.waldman@cuanschutz.edu
  - name: Claude (Opus 4.7, 1M context)
    affiliation: Anthropic
    role: AI co-author (derivation and exposition)
date: today
abstract: |
  Multiple imputation (MI) lets an analyst apply complete-data tools to data
  that were never complete, then combine the results. Many of those tools —
  likelihood-ratio statistics, information criteria, deviance-based fit indices —
  are built on the imputation-averaged complete-data log-likelihood, the
  Q-function. We show that, evaluated at the observed-data estimator and measured
  against the complete-data gold standard, the Q-function carries a systematic
  bias of $+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$, where RIV is the matrix
  relative increase in variance due to missingness. The bias decomposes into two
  mechanisms that do not cancel: an *imputation bias* of $+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$
  from averaging the fit over completions, and an *estimation mismatch* of
  $-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ because MI targets the complete-data
  maximizer rather than the observed-data one. The result holds for any regular
  likelihood model fit on congenially imputed data. We trace its consequences for
  two staples of applied practice. For likelihood-ratio model comparison, the test
  numerator inherits a *differential* bias that vanishes under the null but distorts
  power and model ranking under local alternatives and non-nested comparison — even
  when the reference distribution is calibrated correctly. For information criteria,
  the bias is model-specific, so uncorrected MI-AIC and MI-BIC systematically favor
  candidate models with more missing information; correcting the Q-function restores
  complete-data-equivalent selection. We confirm the identity and both consequences
  with a pre-registered Monte Carlo study. The work sits upstream of the MI
  test-calibration literature: calibration makes the reference distribution right;
  the present correction makes the statistic referred to it centered.
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    embed-resources: true
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    number-sections: true
bibliography: ../literature/mi-spectral-better-bibTeX.bib
---

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

## The problem {#sec-intro-problem}

Multiple imputation replaces each missing entry with several plausible draws, lets
the analyst apply complete-data tools to each completed data set, and combines the
results [@rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987]. Its appeal is a clean division of
labor: the imputer, who may hold information and judgment the analyst lacks, handles
the missingness once; every downstream analyst then works as if the data were
complete [@mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a]. Many of the tools they
reach for — likelihood-ratio statistics, information criteria, profile likelihoods,
deviance-based fit indices — are built, directly or indirectly, on a single object:
the imputation-averaged complete-data log-likelihood, the Q-function familiar from
the EM and congeniality literatures.

Write $\bar Q_\infty(\theta)$ for its idealized infinite-imputation limit. One would
like $\bar Q_\infty$ to behave like the complete-data log-likelihood
$\ell_{\text{com}}$ that would have been computed had nothing been missing. It does
not. Evaluated at the observed-data estimator and measured against the complete-data
gold standard $\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})$, the Q-function is
systematically biased. Anything built on top of it inherits that bias. This paper
derives the bias exactly, to leading order, names the two mechanisms behind it, and
follows it into two settings where applied conclusions hinge on it.

## The result {#sec-intro-result}

Let $\mathrm{RIV} = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1} I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$ denote the matrix
relative increase in variance due to missingness — Rubin's scalar fraction of
missing information in matrix form. Under congenial, proper MI, taking the
complete-data MLE $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$ as the target, the Q-function bias is
$$
\mathbb{E}\big[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big]
= \underbrace{+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})}_{\text{imputation bias}}
\;\;\underbrace{-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})}_{\text{estimation mismatch}}
\;=\; +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}),
$$
to $O(n^{-1})$; on the deviance scale ($-2\bar Q_\infty$), double this. The first
term is **imputation bias**: averaging $\ell_{\text{com}}$ over completions drawn from
the imputation model overstates the fit by $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$. The
second is **estimation mismatch**: MI's estimand is $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$, but
$\bar Q_\infty$ is centered at the observed-data maximizer $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$,
and the gap between the two costs $\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$. The
mechanisms pull in opposite directions but do not cancel; they leave a net upward
bias of half the trace. The statement is general for any regular likelihood model;
we instantiate it throughout for the multivariate normal, and the full proof is given
in a companion derivation document [see @sec-theorem and the appendix derivation].

## Why it matters {#sec-intro-why}

Two applications motivate the paper, and each turns the abstract bias into a concrete
distortion of a decision an analyst actually makes.

**Likelihood-ratio model comparison.** The MI likelihood-ratio numerator
$\hat d_L = 2[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi^*) - \bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi_0^*)]$ — formed, in
the manner of @chanMultipleImprovementsMultiple2022, by maximizing the averaged
log-likelihood rather than averaging maximizers — inherits a *differential* bias,
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}(\hat\psi^*)) - \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}(\hat\psi_0^*))$.
Under the null the two models share the same missing-information structure and the
difference vanishes; under local alternatives or non-nested comparison it does not,
and it distorts power and ranking *even when the reference distribution is calibrated
correctly* [@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022]. Calibration and centering are separate
problems, and the literature has solved only the first.

**Information criteria.** MI-AIC and MI-BIC are built from $-2\bar Q_\infty$, so each
candidate model carries a bias $-\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ that is
*model-specific* — RIV depends on the model. Left uncorrected, the criteria reward a
model for having more missing information, systematically favoring higher-RIV
candidates; correcting the Q-function restores complete-data-equivalent selection.
The direction matches the complete-data discrepancy correction of
@cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a and the missing-data AIC of
@claeskensconsentinoVariableSelectionIncomplete2008, both of which add a positive
trace penalty to discourage exactly this.

## Relation to existing work {#sec-intro-lineage}

The result sits *upstream* of the MI test-calibration literature. Meng and Rubin's
combining rule for likelihood-ratio tests [@mengPerformingLikelihoodRatio1992b], its
refinement by @chanMultipleImprovementsMultiple2022, and Chan's general stacked-MI
tests [@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022] all concern how to *calibrate the reference
distribution* of an MI test statistic, taking the statistic's numerator as given. We
address the *bias of that numerator*. The two are complementary: calibration makes
the reference distribution right; the present correction makes the statistic referred
to it centered. Both are needed to recover complete-data inference under MI.

On the model-selection side, @cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a (AICcd) and
@claeskensconsentinoVariableSelectionIncomplete2008 correct the analogous
complete-data discrepancy under EM-based estimation — a parallel line with a
different target estimator but the same RIV machinery. The connection is in fact
term-by-term: AICcd targets the complete-data discrepancy at the observed-data (EM)
MLE — the same $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ pivot as our imputation-bias term — so it
carries a $+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$-type penalty but no analogue of the
estimation-mismatch term, which is precisely the extra price MI pays for targeting
$\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$ instead of $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$.

## Road map {#sec-intro-roadmap}

@sec-verification describes how the derivation was checked — the procedure we used to
keep an AI-assisted derivation honest, and the evidence a reader can audit.
@sec-setup fixes notation: the model and missingness, the two estimators, the
information matrices and RIV, and the Q-function. @sec-theorem states the
deviance-bias theorem, reads off its two mechanisms, sketches the proof, and points
to the full derivation. @sec-lrt develops the likelihood-ratio application and
@sec-ic the information-criterion application, each paired with the corresponding
Monte Carlo evidence. @sec-discussion discusses scope and open directions.

# Verification: keeping an AI-assisted derivation honest {#sec-verification}

This paper was derived in collaboration with a large language model (see
@sec-provenance). That fact creates a specific obligation. AI systems fail in a
characteristic way — fluently, confidently, and wrong — and a derivation produced this
way is worthless to a reader who cannot tell a real theorem from a plausible-sounding
one. We do not ask the reader to extend trust. Instead we describe the procedure that
constrains the derivation at each point where it could go wrong, and we make the
underlying evidence available to inspect. The aim of this section is to make that
procedure visible, because most of it is ordinarily invisible.

The organizing idea is **triangulation under defense-in-depth**: nothing load-bearing
rests on a single artifact, and the central identity is reached by independent routes
that would have to fail in agreement for the conclusion to be wrong. Concretely, each
distinct way the derivation could be wrong is met by a control aimed at that channel.

**Citations cannot be fabricated.** The most common failure of an AI-assisted
manuscript is a citation that does not say what it is claimed to say, or does not
exist. We close this channel two ways. Mechanically, a pre-write check refuses to save
any change to the manuscript that contains a citation key with no corresponding local
copy of the cited paper; a deliberate placeholder syntax is the only bypass, and it
cannot be mistaken for a real citation. The check enforces that a local copy *exists*.
On top of that mechanism we impose a procedural rule that the author must have read
the local copy in the working session before citing it — reliance on the model's
training-weight memory of a paper is prohibited. References move through an explicit
pipeline (proposed, acquired, read-and-verified, cited), and only verified references
are eligible to appear in the text.

**Source content is pinned, not paraphrased from memory.** Every external claim is
tied to a verbatim quotation from the cited source, carrying the source's own equation
or theorem number and a pointer into the local copy. The full derivation document
reproduces the cited passages in an appendix, so a reader can check each borrowed
result against the original wording rather than against our summary of it.

**The central identity is checked four independent ways.** The bias
$+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ is established by (i) a pen-and-paper proof
(@sec-theorem and the appendix derivation); (ii) an independent *symbolic* check in a
computer-algebra system, which verifies the trace and matrix identities exactly rather
than to within rounding; (iii) an independent numerical reimplementation in a second
language, cross-checked against the original implementation, so an error in either code
base would surface as a disagreement; and (iv) a Monte Carlo study that estimates the
imputation-bias term, the estimation-mismatch term, and their sum across many
configurations and compares them with the analytic half-trace. These routes share no
common machinery; a mistake that survived all four agreeing on the same number is far
less likely than one that slips past any single check.

**Predictions were registered before the data were seen.** The hypotheses for the
Monte Carlo study were written down and frozen at a fixed version of the code *before*
the simulations were run, with the explicit rule that the framing would change if the
results disagreed. This is what separates evidence from storytelling, and it is worth
being concrete about the outcome: the predictions did not all pass. Of eight
pre-registered claims, several were confirmed, several returned as merely observational,
and one — a secondary prediction about the relative size of the bias across two
missingness patterns — failed outright. We report the failure rather than quietly
revising the prediction to fit. The confirmatory core is solid: the simulated
Q-function bias tracks $+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ under congenial,
missing-at-random imputation, and — as the theory's scope requires — departs from it
precisely when congeniality is violated. The full record, including the miss, appears
with the applications in @sec-lrt and @sec-ic.

**The work is attributable.** An ORCID-verified human author directed the research
questions, validated the empirical implications, made all scoping decisions, and
audited the derivation; the model generated derivations and exposition in iterative
dialogue. Provenance is documented in @sec-provenance, consistent with the norms of
the target venue.

None of these controls is novel on its own. What we claim is that, taken together and
applied to an AI-assisted derivation, they convert the reasonable question — *how do I
know this is not machine-generated nonsense?* — into something a reader can settle by
inspection rather than by trust.

# Setup and notation {#sec-setup}

We fix only what is needed to state the theorem. The complete derivation — the full
assumption set, the matrix identities, and the asymptotic lemmas — is given in the
appendix derivation document, which this paper accompanies.

**Data and missingness.** Let $Y$ collect $N$ observations drawn independently from a
regular parametric model $f(\,\cdot\mid\theta)$; we instantiate everything for the
$p$-variate normal, where $\theta=(\mu,\operatorname{vech}\Sigma)$ has dimension
$k = p + \tfrac12 p(p+1)$. A response indicator splits each observation into its
observed and missing parts, $Y=(Y_{\text{obs}},Y_{\text{mis}})$. We assume the
missingness is ignorable — missing at random, with the missingness parameters distinct
from $\theta$ — so the mechanism need not be modeled
[@rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987]. The role of this assumption becomes visible
in @sec-theorem: it is exactly what the theorem needs, and exactly what fails in the
simulations where the bias departs from its predicted value.

**Two estimators.** Missingness forces a distinction the whole argument turns on. Had
$Y$ been observed in full, we would maximize the complete-data log-likelihood
$\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta;Y)$ at the complete-data MLE $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$ — the
gold standard MI aims to recover. From the observed entries alone we can only maximize
the observed-data log-likelihood $\ell_{\text{obs}}(\theta;Y_{\text{obs}})$, at the
observed-data (full-information) MLE $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$. The two maximizers do
not coincide, and the gap between them is the source of one of the two bias terms.

**Three informations.** Each estimator's precision is governed by a Fisher information:
the complete information $I_{\text{com}}$, the observed information $I_{\text{obs}}$,
and their difference, the missing information
$I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}} := I_{\text{com}} - I_{\text{obs}}$. Equivalently,
$$ I_{\text{com}} = I_{\text{obs}} + I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}, \qquad I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}\succeq 0 : $$
missingness can only cost information, never create it
[@schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997].

**Relative increase in variance.** The whole result is written in the ratio of missing
to observed information,
$$ \mathrm{RIV} := I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}\, I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}, $$
Rubin's scalar fraction of missing information in matrix form
[@rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987]. Substituting the identity above gives the
form the trace takes in the theorem,
$$ \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) = \operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}}) - k. $$
(One caution: RIV uses $I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$ on the left, not $I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$; the
latter gives the EM rate / fraction-of-missing-information matrix, a different quantity.)

**Congenial, proper MI and the Q-function.** We assume the imputation and analysis
models are congenial in Meng's sense — loosely, the imputer's posterior moments agree
with the analyst's procedure [@mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a] — and
that the imputations are proper. Rather than maximize $\ell_{\text{obs}}$ directly, MI
averages the complete-data log-likelihood over imputed completions. In the
infinite-imputation limit this average is the Q-function
$$ \bar Q_\infty(\theta) := \mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}}}\!\big[\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta)\big], $$
the EM/Meng Q-function in the proper-MI limit. (This is the averaged *log-likelihood*,
not Rubin's pooled point estimate, which averages complete-data *estimates*.)

**The bias and its split.** The theorem compares $\bar Q_\infty$ at
$\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ against the complete-data gold standard
$\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})$, split at the pivot
$\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ into
$$
\underbrace{A := \bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})}_{\text{imputation bias}},
\qquad
\underbrace{B := \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})}_{\text{estimation mismatch}},
$$
with total bias $T := A + B = \bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})$.
These are on the log-likelihood scale; the deviance scale ($-2\bar Q_\infty$) doubles each.

# The deviance-bias theorem {#sec-theorem}

> **Theorem (Q-function deviance bias).** Under the regularity, ignorability,
> congeniality, and properness conditions of @sec-setup, to leading order
> $$ \mathbb{E}[T] = \mathbb{E}\big[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big] = +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) + O(n^{-1}), $$
> decomposing as $\mathbb{E}[A]=+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})+O(n^{-1})$
> (imputation bias) and $\mathbb{E}[B]=-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})+O(n^{-1})$
> (estimation mismatch). The deviance-scale statement multiplies by two.

The two terms pull in opposite directions but do not cancel. Averaging the
complete-data fit over completions drawn from the imputation model *overstates* the
fit by a full $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$; the fact that MI is centered at
$\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ while its estimand is $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$ *recovers*
half a trace; what is left is a net upward bias of $+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$.

## Proof sketch {#sec-theorem-sketch}

The full proof is given in the appendix derivation document; we sketch its shape here.
The argument pivots on the observed-data estimator $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$, splitting
the total bias into the two terms of @sec-setup and evaluating each in expectation.

**Estimation mismatch ($B$) is elementary.** Both quantities are complete-data
log-likelihoods, so their difference is a second-order Taylor expansion of
$\ell_{\text{com}}$ about its maximizer $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$, whose first-order
term vanishes. Its expectation is
$-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{com}}\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big)$.
The variance of the estimator *gap* — not of $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ itself — is
$I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$, and the trace collapses to
$-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$. (Substituting
$\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})$ for the variance of the gap is the one
natural misstep here; it would manufacture a spurious $-\tfrac12 k$.)

**Imputation bias ($A$) is the delicate half.** After the observed-data parts cancel,
$A$ is the discrepancy between averaging the missing-data log-density under the
imputation draw versus under the truth. A second-order expansion in the imputation
parameter yields three pieces: a leading cross-term that gives
$+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$, and two further $O(1)$ pieces — an entropy-gradient
term and a cross-entropy-curvature term — whose leading contributions cancel to
$O(n^{-1})$. That cancellation is the subtle step (an earlier shortcut wrongly set one
piece to zero by misapplying Gibbs' inequality), and it is isolated and proved as a
lemma. To leading order only the cross-term survives, giving
$\mathbb{E}[A]=+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$.

We are honest about the reach of the cancellation lemma: it is *exact* in closed form
for the families we work through (e.g. the normal and exponential), and the underlying
curvature and order-counting identities supply the general mechanism, so it is
*asserted* for any regular conditional family rather than proved as a closed theorem at
that generality. This is one reason the result is checked by independent routes rather
than rested on the algebra alone (@sec-verification).

## Numerical confirmation {#sec-theorem-mc}

The pre-registered Monte Carlo study estimates the two terms and their sum directly:
it generates complete data, applies a missingness mechanism, imputes, and compares the
realized $\hat T$, $\hat A$, $\hat B$ against the analytic
$\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ computed from the model. Under congenial,
missing-at-random imputation the total tracks the prediction across sample sizes and
both missingness patterns (2{,}000 replicates per cell):

| Pattern | $N$ | $\hat T$ | $\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| monotone | 200 | 2.89 | 2.64 |
| monotone | 500 | 3.06 | 2.63 |
| monotone | 1000 | 2.79 | 2.63 |
| non-monotone | 200 | 4.07 | 3.86 |
| non-monotone | 500 | 3.67 | 3.84 |
| non-monotone | 1000 | 4.40 | 3.83 |

A revealing detail sits underneath this agreement. The individual terms $\hat A$ and
$\hat B$ each run $\sim$10–20% off their leading-order predictions at these sample
sizes — the finite-$n$ residual from the cancellation lemma — yet their *sum* holds
within Monte Carlo error, because the residuals cancel. The theorem is a statement
about $T=A+B$, and that is exactly the quantity the simulation confirms.

The same study locates the theorem's boundary. Its hypotheses assume congeniality;
when we instead impose a not-at-random mechanism that breaks it, $\hat T$ departs from
$\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ sharply and without bound — for the monotone
not-at-random cells $\hat T$ runs from 11 at $N{=}200$ to 45 at $N{=}1000$ against a
prediction near 2.6, diverging rather than converging. This is the expected
behavior, not a failure: the bias identity is a congenial-MI result, and the
simulation shows it holding precisely where it should and failing precisely where it
should.

# Application: likelihood-ratio model comparison {#sec-lrt}

The first place the bias bites is the likelihood-ratio test. Following
@chanMultipleImprovementsMultiple2022, the MI likelihood-ratio numerator is formed by
maximizing the averaged log-likelihood rather than averaging the per-imputation
maximizers,
$$ \hat d_L = 2\big[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi^*) - \bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi_0^*)\big], $$
where $\hat\psi^*$ and $\hat\psi_0^*$ maximize $\bar Q_\infty$ over the full and the
null-restricted parameter space. Each of the two Q-function terms carries the bias of
@sec-theorem, so on the deviance scale the numerator inherits a *differential* bias,
$$ \mathbb{E}[\hat d_L] - d_L^{\text{com}} \;=\; \operatorname{tr}\!\big(\mathrm{RIV}(\hat\psi^*)\big) - \operatorname{tr}\!\big(\mathrm{RIV}(\hat\psi_0^*)\big) + O(n^{-1}). $$

What makes this subtle is *when* it matters. Under the null the unrestricted and
restricted fits coincide asymptotically, the two models share the same
missing-information structure, and the differential bias vanishes. So the null
calibration of @chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022 — which takes the numerator as given and
gets the reference distribution right — is untouched by the bias: a correctly
calibrated test holds its size whether or not the numerator is corrected. The bias is
invisible exactly where size is checked.

Away from the null it reappears. Under local alternatives and in non-nested
comparison the two models carry different missing-information structures, the
differential bias is nonzero, and it distorts the non-centrality of the test —
shifting power and the ranking of models even when the reference distribution is
calibrated correctly. Calibration and centering are genuinely separate problems:
@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022 solves the first, and a test can pass every size check
while still being systematically mis-powered because of the second.

## Numerical confirmation {#sec-lrt-mc}

The pre-registered power study compares, at matched (size-adjusted) level across a grid
of local alternatives, an uncorrected MI likelihood-ratio test against one whose
numerator is bias-corrected. At the pre-registered number of imputations the corrected
test has higher power at every alternative, by a small but consistent margin (roughly
0.5–3 percentage points across the grid). The effect is modest, as the theory predicts
for the differential of two nearby traces, but it is one-directional and stable.

This application is also where pre-registration earned its place. A low-imputation
pilot showed the two tests with indistinguishable power, which we initially read as
evidence that the bias affects only calibration and not power. That reading was wrong:
the apparent tie was Monte Carlo noise swamping a real effect at too few imputations.
Because the prediction had been registered in advance, we ran the larger study rather
than rationalizing the pilot, and the original prediction held. (At few imputations the
correction also buys size, not just power: in the pilot the uncorrected test
over-rejected at 0.07 while the corrected test held 0.04.)

A practical point follows about *how to calibrate* a centered numerator. Centering the
numerator (the present correction) and choosing its reference distribution (Chan's
problem) are separate steps, and once the numerator is bias-corrected one may refer it
either to Chan's Monte Carlo distribution or to a closed-form mean-and-variance
("scaled-shifted") approximation in the style of
@satorraEnsuringPositivenessScaled2010 and @asparouhovRobustChiSquare2006. At the
pre-registered number of imputations the three resulting procedures form a menu rather
than a strict ranking:

| Procedure | Type I ($\alpha=.05$) | Size-adjusted power |
|---|---|---|
| bias-corrected numerator + Chan Monte Carlo | 0.034 (slightly conservative) | highest at every $\delta$ |
| uncorrected numerator + Chan Monte Carlo (Chan as run) | 0.042 (near-nominal) | middle |
| bias-corrected numerator + scaled-shifted reference | 0.045 (nominal) | $\sim$3–5 pp below the first |

Two things are worth reading off this. First, *both* bias-corrected procedures improve
on the uncorrected test exactly as @chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022 runs it — confirming
that centering is a gain on top of correct calibration, not a substitute for it.
Second, the scaled-shifted reference is a *calibration convenience layered on the
centered numerator*, not an alternative to centering: it gives up a few points of
power to Chan's Monte Carlo in exchange for a closed form that needs no simulation. It
is a deliberately approximate, two-moment device — Chan's full reference distribution
is the more accurate calibration — offered for analysts who want the correction's
benefit without the Monte Carlo machinery.

# Application: information-criterion model selection {#sec-ic}

The second application is where the bias does the most damage, because here it is never
checked against a null. Information criteria built for MI — MI-AIC, MI-BIC — score each
candidate model with $-2\bar Q_\infty$ plus a complexity penalty, and pick the smallest.
By @sec-theorem each model's fit term carries a bias of $-\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$
on the deviance scale. The decisive fact is that this bias is *model-specific*: RIV
depends on the model, so different candidates are credited with different,
unearned, amounts of fit. A model with more missing information is rewarded for it. The
criteria therefore have a built-in preference for higher-RIV candidates that has nothing
to do with how well those models describe the data.

This is exactly the distortion that the complete-data discrepancy corrections were built
to remove on the EM side. @cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a (AICcd) and
@claeskensconsentinoVariableSelectionIncomplete2008 both add a positive trace penalty —
$+2\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{oc}}I_{\text{o}}^{-1})$ and an analogous term — precisely
to charge a model back for the missing information it would otherwise be credited with.
Correcting $\bar Q_\infty$ for its $-\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ bias is the MI-side
counterpart: it restores complete-data-equivalent selection.

## Numerical confirmation {#sec-ic-mc}

The pre-registered selection study fixes a true model among candidates that differ in
their missing-information structure and asks how often each criterion recovers it. Three
quantities matter: the selection rate of the uncorrected MI-AIC, the rate after the
Q-function is bias-corrected, and an oracle ceiling — the rate a complete-data-equivalent
criterion would achieve. Under congenial, missing-at-random imputation (FIML engine,
2{,}000 replicates per cell):

| Pattern | $N$ | Uncorrected | Corrected | Oracle ceiling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| monotone | 200 | 0.71 | 0.82 | 0.91 |
| monotone | 500 | 0.74 | 0.83 | 0.90 |
| monotone | 1000 | 0.73 | 0.81 | 0.90 |
| non-monotone | 200 | 0.65 | 0.80 | 0.91 |
| non-monotone | 500 | 0.67 | 0.81 | 0.90 |
| non-monotone | 1000 | 0.67 | 0.81 | 0.90 |

Correcting the Q-function recovers most of the gap between the uncorrected criterion and
the oracle — a 9–15 percentage-point gain in the rate of selecting the true model, larger
where missing information is greater (the non-monotone cells).

The headline is sharper than the rates alone. We also recorded the *direction* of every
mistake: when the uncorrected criterion selected the wrong model, did it favor the
higher-RIV candidate? Across all twelve cells, the answer was yes **100% of the time**.
The bias does not merely add noise to model selection; it pushes selection in a single,
predictable direction — toward whichever model has more missing information — exactly as
the model-specific $-\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ term predicts. This directional
concordance, holding without exception across patterns, sample sizes, and (in the full
study) both imputation engines, is the strongest evidence in the paper that the bias is
real and that its sign is understood.

A closed-form route should reach essentially the same fix. Applying a scaled-shifted
mean-and-variance adjustment [@satorraEnsuringPositivenessScaled2010;
@asparouhovRobustChiSquare2006] to each model's *bias-corrected* deviance difference
against the saturated reference — the same closed-form calibration used for the
likelihood-ratio test in @sec-lrt — recovers the correction's benefit without referring
to $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ explicitly, giving analysts who prefer a single
self-contained statistic a practical equivalent.

::: {.callout-warning title="Draft note — provisional, pending re-run (issue #1)"}
The selection rate for this scaled-shifted arm is **not yet reported** because the
executed W3 simulation applies the adjustment to the *uncorrected* differential deviance
($a\chi^2_{MI}+b$), whereas the intended arm applies it to the *bias-corrected*
differential ($a(\chi^2_{MI}-\text{tr\_perp})+b$), consistent with @sec-lrt. The number
will be regenerated after a one-line fix in `W3-model-selection.R` and `w3-sweep.R`. The
§6 headline (oracle / uncorrected / corrected rates and the 100% directional
concordance) does not involve this arm and is unaffected. See
[issue #1](https://github.com/marcus-waldman/mi-spectral/issues/1).
:::

# Discussion {#sec-discussion}

One identity drives the paper: evaluated at the observed-data estimator and measured
against the complete-data gold standard, the MI Q-function is biased upward by
$\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$, the residue of an imputation bias of
$+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ and an estimation mismatch of
$-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ that do not cancel. The two applications are
the same bias read in two registers. In likelihood-ratio testing it appears as a
*difference* of traces that vanishes under the null — so it never shows up in a size
check — and distorts power off the null. In information criteria it appears in *levels*,
model by model, and silently rewards candidates with more missing information; this is
the application where the cost is largest and least visible, and where the correction
matters most.

**Calibration and centering are separate problems.** It is worth stating plainly how
this work relates to the MI testing literature it builds on. The combining rule of
@mengPerformingLikelihoodRatio1992b and its successors, culminating in
@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022, are about *calibration*: given a test numerator, what is
the right reference distribution to refer it to? That problem is solved, and we do not
reopen it. The present work is about *centering*: is the numerator being referred to
that distribution itself unbiased? The two are orthogonal and both are necessary. A
test can be perfectly calibrated and still systematically mis-powered because its
numerator is off-center; an unbiased numerator referred to the wrong distribution is no
better. Recovering complete-data inference under MI requires both.

**Scope and limitations.** The result is a congenial-MI result, and we have been
deliberate about showing where that boundary lies: the bias identity holds under
ignorable, congenial, proper imputation and breaks — visibly and without bound — when
congeniality fails (@sec-theorem-mc). It is stated for regular likelihood models, and
the proof is general in form, but we instantiate and verify it throughout for the
multivariate normal; we do not claim to have exercised it across model families, and
the most delicate step of the imputation-bias argument (the cancellation lemma) is exact
in closed form for the families we work through and asserted, via its general mechanism,
beyond them. The empirical evidence rests on a single data-generating family explored
across missingness patterns, sample sizes, and imputation engines, not on a broad survey
of models. None of these is a gap in the central claim; they mark the edges of what has
been demonstrated as opposed to argued.

**A conjecture.** The power study shows the bias-corrected likelihood-ratio test
dominating the uncorrected one at matched size across the alternatives we examined. We
suspect this is an instance of a more general power ordering — that correcting the
numerator cannot lose power at matched level and strictly gains it whenever the two
models' missing-information structures differ — but we have only partial empirical
support, not a proof, and we leave the general statement as a conjecture.

**Open directions.** The most pressing is uncongeniality. Much applied MI is
uncongenial in Meng's sense [@mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a] —
predictive mean matching is the obvious case — and there the variance-recovery identity
the proof leans on no longer holds; the simulations already show $T$ departing from
$\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ once congeniality is broken, so an uncongenial
treatment needs different machinery, not a patched constant. Non-regular settings
(parameters on a boundary, mixtures) sit outside the regularity conditions entirely. And
the relationship to the EM-based discrepancy corrections of
@cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a and
@claeskensconsentinoVariableSelectionIncomplete2008 — which target the same RIV
machinery at a different estimator — deserves a unified treatment that we have only
sketched here.

**For practice.** The correction is cheap. Where $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ can
be estimated — and the MI machinery that produces between- and within-imputation
variances already estimates it — subtracting the model-specific bias from each
candidate's information-criterion score is a small addition that restores
complete-data-equivalent selection; a closed-form moment-matching variant gives a
serviceable approximation when imputations are few. The practical message is narrow and
actionable: an analyst comparing models on multiply imputed data with an
information criterion should not compare raw $-2\bar Q_\infty$ scores across models with
different fractions of missing information, because the comparison is tilted toward the
higher-RIV model before the data speak.

A companion paper develops the same correction for structural-equation modeling, where
the Q-function underlies the fit statistics used for goodness-of-fit testing and model
comparison; it cites the present derivation and does not duplicate it.

# Provenance {#sec-provenance .unnumbered}

This manuscript is targeted at [JAIGP](https://jaigp.org) (Journal for AI Generated
Papers), which requires transparent attribution of AI involvement.

**Authorship model.** The derivation was developed in collaboration between Marcus
Waldman (ORCID-verified human prompter) and Claude (Anthropic, Opus 4.7). Waldman
directed the research questions, validated the empirical implications, and made all
scoping decisions. The mathematical derivations, expository structure, and proof
sketches were generated by Claude in iterative dialogue and audited by Waldman. The
verification procedure that disciplines this collaboration is described in
@sec-verification.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
